interactions.summary: Jiang completes Virgil's theory of love: desire is natural and beauty inflames the soul, but ethical freedom consists in judging, curbing, and resisting bad love.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang interpreting Virgil's account of love and free will.

interactions.summary: Jiang asks students to recall Dante's answer, then states it: true love wants the beloved's happiness and dignity rather than possession.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang with a classroom prompt; student response is not captured.

interactions.summary: He turns to Purgatory's earthquake and Statius, setting up the difference between hell and purgatory as a matter of will and willingness to do penance.

speaker_notes.note: The repeated 'gathers to the earth' phrase is likely ASR damage in quoted philosophical material; the stable point is Jiang's distinction between natural longing and chosen assent.

claims.claim: In Virgil's model, love is not automatically praiseworthy; it depends on the object and on whether the beloved or desired action is good.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's reading of Virgil's ethics of love.

claims.claim: Virgil answers the free-will problem by saying humans may not choose the first inflaming desire, but they possess the power to judge and curb that love.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's reading of Virgil's free-will account.

claims.claim: Dante's answer, in Jiang's framing, is that true love wants what is best for the beloved and refuses to degrade the beloved into a price or object.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture contrast between Dantean and Virgilian love.

claims.claim: Jiang defines the difference between hell and purgatory as will: purgatorial sinners admit sin and commit to penance; hell's sinners refuse to admit wrong.

signature_moments.moment: Virgil's ethics reduces freedom to the power to curb love.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the power to curb that love is still your own
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the cleanest contrast between restraint-based morality and Dante's later love-as-respect model.

signature_moments.moment: Dantean love refuses to turn the beloved into a transaction.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the force of Jiang's answer to Virgil's possession model.

signature_moments.moment: The difference between hell and purgatory is not the sin but the will to admit wrong.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The difference is the question of will
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This becomes the hinge for why Statius can rise and Virgil cannot.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Virgil's free-will model: humans can resist or deny bad love even if desire first arises naturally.
Love that seeks the beloved's happiness and dignity rather than possession.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang asks students to remember Dante's response, but no substantive student answer is audible in the transcript.
